name: LLM01: Prompt Injection
description: Attacker crafting malicious prompts can manipulate LLM to ignore system instructions, bypass safety filters, or execute unintended commands, compromising application security.
mitigations: Implement strict input validation and sanitization
Use privilege separation between system and user prompts
Apply output filtering and content moderation
Employ prompt engineering with clear boundaries

name: LLM02: Insecure Output Handling
description: Application trusting LLM output without validation can lead to XSS, SSRF, or command injection when outputs are used in downstream systems.
mitigations: Treat LLM output as untrusted user input
Implement output encoding and sanitization
Use sandboxing for code execution
Apply least privilege principles for downstream actions

name: LLM03: Training Data Poisoning
description: Attacker injecting malicious data into training corpus can cause model to produce biased, harmful, or backdoored outputs.
mitigations: Implement data provenance and validation
Use anomaly detection on training data
Apply data sanitization and filtering
Maintain secure data sourcing practices

name: LLM04: Model Denial of Service
description: Attacker sending resource-intensive prompts can exhaust model resources causing service degradation, high costs, or complete unavailability.
mitigations: Implement rate limiting per user/API key
Set input length and complexity limits
Use request queuing and timeout mechanisms
Monitor resource usage and costs

name: LLM05: Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
description: Using compromised third-party models, datasets, or plugins can introduce vulnerabilities, backdoors, or malicious functionality into LLM applications.
mitigations: Verify checksums and signatures of all components
Use trusted sources for models and data
Conduct security audits of dependencies
Maintain software bill of materials (SBOM)

name: LLM06: Sensitive Information Disclosure
description: LLM can leak sensitive information from training data, system prompts, or user conversations through its responses.
mitigations: Implement data loss prevention (DLP) on outputs
Use data anonymization in training
Apply output filtering for PII and secrets
Maintain separate models for sensitive domains

name: LLM07: Insecure Plugin Design
description: Poorly designed LLM plugins can allow privilege escalation, unauthorized data access, or remote code execution through plugin interfaces.
mitigations: Implement strict plugin authorization
Use sandboxing for plugin execution
Validate all plugin inputs and outputs
Apply principle of least privilege

name: LLM08: Excessive Agency
description: LLM with excessive permissions or autonomy can perform unintended actions, causing damage through overly broad tool access or decision-making authority.
mitigations: Implement human-in-the-loop for critical actions
Use granular permission controls
Apply action confirmation mechanisms
Maintain audit logging of all actions

name: LLM09: Overreliance
description: Users or systems trusting LLM outputs without verification can act on hallucinated or incorrect information, leading to poor decisions or security incidents.
mitigations: Implement output verification mechanisms
Display confidence scores to users
Require human validation for critical decisions
Educate users on LLM limitations

name: LLM10: Model Theft
description: Attacker through API queries or direct access can extract or replicate proprietary LLM models, stealing intellectual property and enabling derivative attacks.
mitigations: Implement query rate limiting and monitoring
Use model watermarking techniques
Apply API access controls and authentication
Monitor for systematic model probing
